items.front: When may radar services be provided?
items.back: Only when the controller is personally satisfied that the radar presentation and equipment performance are adequate for the service being provided.

items.front: Whose judgment controls whether the radar presentation is adequate for the service being provided?
items.back: The controller providing the service.

items.front: What must be done when radar malfunctions or unexpected outages occur?
items.back: Notify the OS/CIC and advise adjacent facilities when appropriate.

items.front: Are radar services limited to the distance and altitude parameters obtained during the commissioning flight check?
items.back: No.

items.front: What two factors must be adequate before radar service is provided?
items.back: The radar presentation and the equipment performance.

items.front: If the radar presentation becomes intermittent and no longer seems adequate, what is the correct response?
items.back: Do not continue treating it as adequate radar service and notify the OS/CIC of the malfunction.

items.front: What surveillance sources should be used?
items.back: Approved ATC surveillance sources.

items.front: In terminal FUSION mode, when does the standard source-use restriction still apply?
items.back: Only when required by facility directive.

items.front: When may secondary-radar-only targets be used to conduct surveillance final approaches?
items.back: Only when an emergency exists and the pilot concurs.

items.front: What advisory describes a temporary primary-radar outage with secondary-only service availability?
items.back: Primary radar unavailable. Radar services available on transponder or ADS-B equipped aircraft only.

items.front: What terminal modes permit ADS-B and WAM targets to support all terminal services, including services for procedures annotated radar required?
items.back: STARS Fusion, STARS FMA, and STARS Multi-Sensor Mode.

items.front: What WAM limitation applies in the EAS?
items.back: WAM-derived targets cannot be used to provide 3 NM separation in the EAS.

items.front: When may the primary-radar-unavailable advisory be omitted?
items.back: When it is provided on ATIS or by other appropriate notice to pilots as allowed by the paragraph.

items.front: Where are all EA activity requests referred?
items.back: The appropriate center supervisor.

items.front: If EA activity interferes with en route radar use, who is asked to suspend it?
items.back: The responsible military unit or aircraft, if the initial request was received directly from the pilot.

items.front: If EA activity interferes with terminal radar use, what is the normal path for requesting suspension?
items.back: Through the ARTCC.

items.front: When may a terminal facility broadcast a stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency?
items.back: When immediate cessation of the activity is required.

items.front: Before a terminal facility broadcasts a resume clearance directly to the EA aircraft, what must be obtained?
items.back: ARTCC approval.

items.front: What items belong in each EA stop request?
items.back: The facility name, the type of EA activity, the radar band affected, and when feasible the expected duration of suspension.

items.front: What phraseology stops buzzer activity on a named band or channel?
items.back: Big Photo ... stop buzzer on (frequency band or channel).

items.front: Which aircraft receive merging target procedures, except while established in a holding pattern?
items.back: Radar-identified aircraft at 10,000 feet and above, turbojet aircraft regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft regardless of altitude.

items.front: When is traffic information issued under the merging target rule?
items.back: When covered aircraft targets appear likely to merge, unless they are separated by more than the appropriate vertical separation minima.

items.front: In RVSM airspace, what extra condition triggers vectoring to prevent targets from merging when the aircraft are 1,000 feet apart?
items.back: Either pilot reports being unable RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave.

items.front: If the pilot requests a vector to avoid merging targets, what should ATC do?
items.back: Vector the aircraft if able and inform the pilot if unable to provide vector service.

items.front: Why should traffic be issued early in a merging-target situation?
items.back: Because aircraft closure rates can be rapid, so the pilot needs time to decide whether a vector is necessary.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to issue a vector for merging traffic avoidance?
items.back: Fly heading two niner zero, vector for traffic.

items.front: When must radar surveillance of an outer-fix holding pattern airspace area be provided?
items.back: Whenever aircraft are holding there and the area, or a portion of it, is shown on the radar scope.

items.front: What type of holding area does this rule address?
items.back: Outer-fix holding pattern airspace areas or portions of them shown on the radar scope.

items.front: What should the controller attempt to detect while aircraft are holding there?
items.back: Any aircraft that stray outside the area.

items.front: If an aircraft is detected straying outside the holding pattern airspace area, what should ATC do?
items.back: Assist it to return to the assigned airspace.

items.front: Does this surveillance rule apply to portions of the holding area shown on the scope, or only the full pattern?
items.back: It applies to the area or any portions of it that are shown on the scope.
